tweets.text:
Elite admissions select for one trait: getting the known answer faster than anyone else. 18 years of optimizing against an answer key someone already wrote.

AI just made the answer key free. Everyone has it instantly now.

So the kids trained hardest to win spent their whole lives mastering the one thing that's now a commodity. The premium moved to the questions with no answer key yet. 

We need a new training. 

The new training is about one thing:

How to be the first person standing in a new land, exploring it, preparing it for the coming billion people who will need it. The future will be built by these people. 

And there is a lot to build.

MSTR from an investor's perspective is extremely risky as a result of the senior claims and its large premium to liquidation value.

Common equity issuance at any price is always credit positive to the capital structure, which benefits credit and senior equity holders at the expense of common equity holders.

The purchase of BTC with its cash reserves, by a company that generates severely negative cash flow equal to a ~4%+ annual common equity dilution rate and is solely reliant on capital markets financing to support its debt service and preferred equity obligations, is not credit positive.

Inflationary pressures are rising in Japan:

Japan's PPI inflation rose +0.9% MoM in May, the 2nd-highest reading since October 2022.

Furthermore, April's reading was revised higher by +0.5 percentage points, to +2.8%, the highest since 2014.

YoY, producer prices surged +6.3%, the biggest annual increase since March 2023.

This was driven by surging costs across petroleum and coal products, electricity, gas, and chemicals since the start of the Iran War.

Rising input costs are now increasing pressure on Japanese firms to pass higher prices on to consumers, raising the risk of broader inflationary pressures.

This strengthens the case for the Bank of Japan to proceed with a rate hike this week, with additional hikes possible later in the year if energy prices remain elevated.

Inflation in Japan is accelerating.

tweets.text:
All of evolution is taking what isn’t yours. 

You take wood to build houses. 
You take plants to eat. 
You take seeds to farm.
You take land for agriculture.
You take metals for production.
You take animals for meat.
You take people for labour.
You take oil for energy. 

To survive requires taking what isn’t yours. To thrive requires systematizing it. 

Civilisation as a concept came to be to normalize taking and laying out protections for doing so.

Today, we call it economics.

tweets.text:
To deal or not to deal. That has been the market's question for months.

Whether the current conflict continues or a deal is reached to reopen Hormuz will have major implications for #gold, #silver, #uranium, and #oil.

My Sunday thoughts:

I believe some form of agreement will eventually be reached. Both sides want to appear strong for domestic audiences, but both also need a deal.

Iran needs to sell oil. Trump needs lower inflation and lower interest rates ahead of the midterm elections.

If the conflict drags on, oil prices could remain elevated for longer, keeping inflation sticky and delaying rate cuts. Continued liquidity stress could weigh on precious metals in the short term.

If Hormuz reopens, I expect oil to fall sharply. The USD would likely weaken, and gold and silver could stage a strong rebound.

One interesting observation: the uranium long-term price has not been affected by this crisis.

Since late January, gold and silver have seen significant corrections, while the uranium long-term price has actually risen. Uranium equities, however, have been sold off alongside the broader commodity sector. If precious metals recover, I would expect uranium stocks to participate.

There is another important trend developing: the world is adapting.

Despite fears of a Hormuz disruption, oil prices never reached the extreme levels many expected. Production is increasing in some regions, alternative transport routes are being utilized, new infrastructure is being built, and demand destruction is appearing in parts of Asia.

When Hormuz fully reopens, the oil market could move from perceived scarcity toward surplus faster than many expect. Restocking may provide temporary support, but I would not be surprised to see oil trade significantly lower by autumn.

That's one reason I have chosen not to buy oil stocks despite considering it when Hormuz was closed. The risk/reward doesn't appeal to me.

For precious metals and uranium, however, the long-term story remains intact.

Global debt continues to rise. Governments everywhere are running large deficits. History suggests that eventually leads to currency debasement and renewed demand for hard assets.

Silver already faces a structural supply deficit while industrial demand continues to grow.

Uranium may be the clearest case of all.

Unlike most commodities, uranium demand is relatively easy to model. Nuclear reactors consume uranium. Count the reactors operating today, those under construction, and those planned, then compare that demand with expected mine supply.

The problem is that very few large uranium mines are currently under construction or construction-ready, and new mines often require 10–20 years to move from discovery to production.

Much higher uranium prices are ultimately required to incentivize the next generation of mines.

In my view, the market has delayed this adjustment for too long. As utilities increasingly recognize future supply risks, competition for pounds could become intense.

I remain extremely bullish on uranium, gold, and silver over the coming years. Uranium >$300, gold >$10,000 and silver >$200 in the not too distand future wouldn't surprise me at all.

Just my thoughts. Please do your own DD.

Follow me for regular market updates and visit my profile to see my top picks.

thread_tweets.text:
There is no such thing as a scientific “method” in the sense of a reliable algorithm or repeatable process. 

We’re taught in grade school that a scientist tabulates observations, then induces a theory from those observations, and then tests them to get to the truth. 

But observations are themselves mini-theories (you never observe anything directly), induction from observations can’t give you novel theories (black swan problem), and testing can only eliminate competing theories, not prove them (error correction).

All you’re left with is creative guesswork and then eliminating the bad guesses to get you closer to the truth, but with no finality. Not much of a process or method. 

“Scientists” have no special method or process that they employ. They’re doing what all of us do all day long to navigate the world, hopefully with more rigor and dedication, but it’s the exact same process at its core.

What made the scientific revolution possible was a philosophical shift in which parts of western society created social conventions around rigorous truth seeking rather than continuing to justify the beliefs of the ancients. 

See Deutsch’s “The Beginning of Infinity” and Karl Popper’s writing, “On the Non-Existence of Scientific Method."

Wall Street digests record fundraising haul as AI race intensifies. Companies have raised roughly $4.7tn across global equity, debt and bank loan markets this year, a record pace, acc to data provider LSEG. That figure, up 7% YoY, does not include the spurt of activity in investment-grade private credit markets, which are increasingly being tapped to finance data centres, chips and power plants feeding the AI boom. That included a $35bn debt package cobbled together by Apollo and Blackstone this week for Anthropic.
 https://t.co/sZzqDyyGvS

From "AI regulation is coming" to "AI regulation is here"

From Mexico border to global border

Fable/Mythos 5 ban was a wake up call for everybody
> China been promoting open intelligence so obviously they're getting more love
> Europe has been slacking and now they have to wake up and build their own intelligence
> Enterprises now have to think twice about sticking w/ 1 provider. Because if agents are integrated across internal workflows, what happen when the gov turn that off?
> Talents will prob have 3 choices — learn Mandarin, build sovereign AI for their country, or build open-source/decentralized intelligence

OpenRouter Fusion proves Fable 5 level of intelligence is achievable by aggregating open models together

Whatever happens, open & decentralized AI wins

Sharing my thoughts on this + relfections/takeaways from SuperAI in today's the After Hour EP.59

This week's Video:

The AI Mid-Cycle Slowdown: OpenAI, Anthropic, and the AI Price War

As Gavin Baker said on BG2 - “You've had a lot of stocks that forget climbing a mountain or hill. They've gone straight up a cliff.”

“They're tired. They need to rest.”

At the same time, the only people doubting AI are the perma-bears which means a two-sided consolidation is likely from here.

The easy part of the trade is over.

The next phase is about price compression at the model layer, rising costs at the physical layer but with continued rotation away from the spenders (hyperscalers) toward the companies receiving the capex.

This is not bubble collapse.

It is digestion.

Watch here:https://t.co/Z5JwstESCF
